lesson.menu_title: Persuading the Client -- The Second Conditional
lesson.menu_desc: Persuade a hesitant client on the phone using the second conditional for hypothetical offers: if we lowered the price, you would order more; if you signed today, we would guarantee delivery -- past simple in the if-clause, would + verb in the result -- 28 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 17 -- Persuading the Client: The Second Conditional
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Roberto Rezende | Aula 17 -- Persuading the Client: The Second Conditional

listenings.text: Hi Roberto, this is Amy. I am calling to brief you before your call with the client this afternoon. Here is the situation. They love the engines, but they are hesitant about the price. The deal is not lost, but they need a reason to commit today. So this is a moment to persuade, not to push. The best tool here is the second conditional. You imagine a result that depends on a condition. For example, you could say: if we lowered the price by five percent, you would save a lot on a large order. Or: if you signed the contract today, we would guarantee delivery in March. Notice the shape. In the if part, use the past simple, lowered, signed, ordered. In the result part, use would plus the base verb, you would save, we would guarantee. It sounds polite and professional, because you are not demanding anything. You are offering a worthwhile trade. Try to make it a win-win. Offer a small incentive, like a free trial or a faster delivery, in exchange for a bigger commitment. Stay calm, sound confident, and let the conditional do the work. Good luck on the call, and tell me how it goes.

This is a quick summary of my call with the client this afternoon. They were still hesitant about the price, so I did not push. Instead, I used the second conditional to offer a few hypothetical deals. I told them that if they ordered five hundred units, we would give them a better discount. I also said that if they signed before Friday, we would guarantee delivery in March and add a free trial of the new model. They liked the idea of a win-win. The free trial was a strong incentive, and the delivery guarantee removed their main concern. In the end, I think I managed to persuade them. They said they would discuss it internally and confirm by Friday. It felt natural to use would and the past simple to make the offers, and the client never felt pushed. I will send Amy the full notes this evening, and I am confident this is going to close.

extra_audio.text: First, listen carefully and understand why the client is hesitant. Then, acknowledge their concern so they feel heard. After that, offer a hypothetical deal using the second conditional, if you ordered more, we would lower the price. Next, add a small incentive to make it a win-win. Finally, confirm the next step and let them think it over.
